one_line_takeaway: A rigorous first taxonomy of 533 real Solidity compiler bugs showing that its most common and most security-dangerous defects are silent semantic-analysis and formal-verification errors that existing crash-oracle fuzzers almost entirely miss (14/108 benchmark bugs).
key_finding: Solidity compiler bugs are dominated by semantic analysis errors (156/533) and formal verification errors (75/533) - together 43.40% of all bugs - and 30.02% of bugs are non-crash (silent). The three evaluated fuzzers are ineffective: they found only 1 crash in the latest solc and 14 of the 108 benchmark bugs total, detecting just 1 of 24 benchmarked semantic analysis errors and 5 of 36 formal verification errors, because they lack scope/type/data-flow-aware mutators and rely solely on a crash oracle. Formal verification errors (a component unique to solc) surged after 2020, reaching 31.57% of bugs in the last studied year vs 14.07% overall.
core_contribution: The first systematic empirical study of bugs in the Solidity compiler (solc). From 1,210 GitHub issues labeled 'bug' (closed on or before Oct 16 2023), the authors filter to 533 unique, dedicated-fix bugs and hand-classify each into a taxonomy of 5 symptoms and 12 root causes, deriving 7 'takeaways' about how bugs manifest, what code patterns/compilation flags trigger them, and what test oracles are needed. They then build an extensible benchmark of 108 reproducible crash bugs spanning 36 buggy compiler release versions and empirically evaluate three off-the-shelf Solidity compiler fuzzers on it, showing they are weak at detecting compiler bugs. This is the paper's OWN contribution; conventional-compiler and DL-compiler bug studies (Sun et al., Shen et al., Chaliasos et al., Zhou et al.) are cited only as related work the authors argue does NOT transfer to Solidity because of solc's unique components (formal verification, Yul, gas optimization).
threat_model: Not an attacker-vs-defender paper, but genuinely security-motivated. The security relevance is that solc can emit unsafe bytecode even from correct source: the paper opens with a compiler flaw that silently turned a correct source-level reentrancy guard into a broken one in bytecode, undetected for two years until a July 2023 exploit causing ~$73.5M loss. It later analyzes the Solidity team's list of 77 known security-relevant compiler bugs, noting ALL 77 are non-crash bugs and 24 require the legacy optimizer, Yul optimizer, or ABI encoder/decoder to reproduce; it uses the Keccak-256 optimizer caching bug as a concrete case where an optimization produces a hash collision an attacker can use to bypass security checks. The paper's thesis is that because these dangerous bugs are silent (non-crash), current crash-oracle fuzzers miss them, and better oracles/mutators are needed.
limitations: Empirical-study threats the authors acknowledge: external validity (representativeness of GitHub-issue bugs; they mitigate by taking all labeled issues but necessarily exclude bugs without a dedicated fix or clear description, and unreproducible/non-crash bugs are excluded from the fuzzing benchmark) and internal validity (subjectivity of manual labeling, mitigated by cross-validation + expert). The fuzzer evaluation benchmark is limited to 108 crash-symptom bugs precisely because the three fuzzers have no oracle for non-crash symptoms, so it cannot measure detection of the very silent/security-relevant bugs the paper argues matter most. Only three AFL-derived fuzzers were tested. 'Fuzzing-by-proxy' (fuzz latest, replay on old versions) is an approximation of true per-version fuzzing. Findings are specific to solc and may not generalize to other smart-contract languages/compilers (e.g., Vyper).

datasets_used: 1,210 GitHub issues labeled 'bug' in the ethereum/solidity repo, closed on or before Oct 16 2023, filtered to 533 unique analyzable bugs
Constructed benchmark: 108 reproducible crash-symptom bugs across 36 buggy solc release versions (released with the paper)
Solidity team's public 'List of Known Bugs': 77 security-relevant compiler bugs referenced in the security discussion
Seed pools for fuzzing: 5,812 test cases / 25,305 fragments / 39,978 templates (AFL-compiler-fuzzer), 308 test cases (solfuzzer), 1,466 test cases (Fuzzol)
quantitative_results: 533 bugs total classified into 5 symptoms and 12 root causes
Symptom distribution: Crash 373 (Valid Program Rejection 241 + Uninformative Error Message 132), Incorrect Output 103, Error Omission 42, Performance Issue 7, Hang 8

Root-cause distribution: Semantic Analysis 156, Formal Verification 75, Code Generation 59, Type System 52, Exception Handling 51, Memory-Related 44, Optimization 35, Syntactic Analysis 30, Doc 10, Read-write 9, Yul Analysis 6, Others 6
30.02% of the 533 bugs do NOT result in a crash; 24.76% are Uninformative Error Message; semantic + formal verification errors = 43.40% of all bugs

Fuzzing results (Table 2, 20-day runs): AFL-compiler-fuzzer 1 crash in latest solc, 8 benchmark bugs, 80.12% map density; solfuzzer 0 crashes, 0 bugs, 88.22%; Fuzzol 0 crashes, 5 bugs, 81.00%; Fuzzol with AFL-compiler-fuzzer seeds +1 additional bug; total 14 of 108 benchmark bugs detected
Fuzzers detected only 1 of 24 benchmarked semantic analysis errors and 5 of 36 formal verification errors, but 5 of 12 memory-related errors
Inter-rater agreement (Cohen's Kappa) rose to 72% after 100 bugs and stayed >95% after 150 bugs

Formal verification errors = 31.57% of bugs in the last studied year (Oct 2022-Oct 2023) vs 14.07% across all bugs; 11.63% of all bugs triggered by inline assembly; all 77 security-relevant known bugs are non-crash, 24 need legacy/Yul optimizer or ABI encoder/decoder
baselines_compared: AFL-compiler-fuzzer (AFL + language-agnostic mutation and code-composition rules)
solfuzzer (AFL-based fuzzer bundled with solc that varies compilation flags)
Fuzzol (AFL-based fuzzer with Solidity AST-aware, language-specific mutators)
